acceptance_criteria.description: Given a fixture recall (seeded temp store or a stub `recall` on PATH returning known JSON), a wm.fleet.recall.query request for a term present in the fixture yields a result with expected hit(s) having id, kind, subject, score, and a bounded snippet (embedded NATS).
Result payload is size-bounded: snippets are truncated to the configured max length and hit count never exceeds the requested (and cap-clamped) limit; truncated:true set when clamping occurred.
wm-tether-recall query (requester) prints ranked hits in a format matching recall query's columns for the fixture case (golden-compared).
The responder refuses a mutating op: a request carrying a write/forget verb is rejected with an error reply and the fixture store is unchanged (grep-assertable: no write path wired).
Requester timeout: with no responder present, wm-tether-recall query exits non-zero within the bounded timeout and does not hang.
End-to-end round-trip selftest: requester -> wm.fleet.recall.query -> responder -> wm.fleet.recall.result.<req_id> -> requester prints hits, exactly one reply consumed per request (req_id matched).
cargo test green; sigpipe::reset() first in main() (grep-asserted); subprocess invocation uses no shell metacharacters (grep-asserted: no sh -c / string-interpolated command line).

ambiguities_resolved.question: AC6 is marked deferred in the PRD (deferred_acs: [6]). How to handle it?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Implement AC6 as a mock test in tests/mocks/ac6.rs exercising the same public API surface (round-trip request/response) against an in-process channel pair or embedded NATS server, satisfying the hardware-mock convention. The deferred_acs:[6] frontmatter means embedded real NATS end-to-end with a live bus is deferred; the mock proves the contract.

ambiguities_resolved.question: What is the embedded NATS test strategy?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Use async-nats with its embedded server option (or nats-server in a test subprocess) for AC1-AC5 integration tests. For AC6 mock, use in-process channel simulation to avoid depending on an installed nats-server.

five_whys_trace.a: When jsy is at the work node, wintermute has no access to the laptop's recall memory store, so the AI assistant is amnesiac about everything it has learned.
Replication requires a write-sync model with conflict resolution, which is complex and risky for a memory store. Read-only bridging delivers most of the value with none of the risk.
five_whys_trace.q: Why not replicate recall to the work node?

five_whys_trace.a: The fleet bus (agorabus on NATS) is already the established wintermute inter-machine communication layer. Reusing it means no new auth/firewall rules, and other fleet tools already speak this protocol.
five_whys_trace.q: Why use NATS/agorabus as the transport instead of SSH tunneling or a REST API?

five_whys_trace.a: The PRD explicitly says 'reuse the daemon/CLI surface, not re-implement query ranking.' The recall binary already has FTS5 + vector ranking wired; a subprocess call is the safe, maintenance-free bridge that can't desync with recall's internal implementation.
five_whys_trace.q: Why invoke the recall CLI as a subprocess instead of linking against its library?

non_goals: Write-back / cross-machine mutations to the recall store (deferred to follow-on PRD)
Re-implementing recall's query ranking logic (bridge reuses recall CLI)
Embedding or replacing the recall daemon
Web UI or HTTP API surface
root_motivation: The self's memory (recall) is strictly local; when jsy is at the work node wintermute is amnesiac. The root motivation is to make the laptop's recall store legible from the work node — read-only first, no write-conflict risk — by routing queries over the fleet bus (agorabus/NATS). This is the minimum bridging step before full fleet memory sync.
scope: A wm-tether-recall binary (single crate) that acts as a NATS fleet bus bridge for recall queries
Responder side: subscribes to wm.fleet.recall.query, invokes recall CLI subprocess (no shell), replies on wm.fleet.recall.result.<req_id>
Requester side: wm-tether-recall query <text> [--kind K] [--limit N] [--hybrid] publishes request, waits for result, prints hits
wm-tether-recall status command reporting responder reachability and last round-trip time
Allowlist of read-only operations; refusal of any write/forget verb
Size-bounded results: snippet length cap + limit cap
Bounded timeout on requester side with clean non-zero exit
Tests using fixture recall (stub recall shim on PATH returning known JSON)
user_persona: jsy at the work node running wm-tether-recall query to query the laptop's recall store, and the laptop's responder daemon running to serve those queries back over the NATS fleet bus.
